evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:CDC West Nile Virus Key Messages
assignment_basis: CDC explicitly describes humans as dead-end hosts for WNV.
review_notes: Review only if assignment vocabulary changes.

evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:WHO West Nile virus fact sheet
assignment_basis: WHO explicitly describes horses as dead-end hosts for WNV.
review_notes: Common-name horse source matched to domestic horse candidate.

evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:CDC Transmission of Yellow Fever Virus
assignment_basis: CDC describes humans as part of the urban human-mosquito transmission cycle.
review_notes: Cycle-specific urban role; do not use as sylvatic reservoir evidence.

evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:CDC Transmission of Yellow Fever Virus
assignment_basis: CDC describes non-human primates as primary vertebrate hosts in sylvatic yellow fever maintenance.
review_notes: Group-level assignment only; implemented as a broad proxy for non-human primate roster rows, not source-backed species-level evidence.

evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:WHO Dengue and severe dengue fact sheet
assignment_basis: WHO describes human-to-mosquito transmission during dengue viremia.
review_notes: Urban transmission role; no non-human reservoir assignment made.

assignment_basis: CDC describes natural plague maintenance in a wild rodent-flea cycle.
review_notes: Group-level assignment only; do not propagate to every rodent species.

assignment_basis: CDC describes humans as incidental hosts during plague epizootic spillover.
review_notes: Human-to-human pneumonic transmission is a separate context and not a reservoir role.

evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:WHO Rift Valley fever fact sheet
assignment_basis: WHO describes amplification in naive ruminants via local competent mosquitoes.
review_notes: Group-level livestock assignment only.

evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:WHO Rift Valley fever fact sheet
assignment_basis: WHO describes RVF as primarily affecting animals but also infecting humans.
review_notes: Spillover/incidental human role.

assignment_basis: CDC Yellow Book identifies viremic humans as likely main amplifying reservoirs for mosquito infection.
review_notes: Urban-cycle human amplification.

assignment_basis: CDC describes infected people as sources for urban Aedes transmission.
review_notes: Urban-cycle human amplification.

assignment_basis: StatPearls describes sylvatic rodents as primary reservoir hosts for enzootic strains.
review_notes: Group-level assignment only.

evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:CDC MMWR Update Venezuelan Equine Encephalitis Colombia 1995
assignment_basis: CDC describes equines as the most important vertebrate amplifying host in the epizootic cycle.
review_notes: Common equine evidence mapped to domestic horse candidate.

evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:CDC Emerging Infectious Diseases CCHF Virus in Cattle and Ticks Israel
assignment_basis: CDC EID article describes livestock as asymptomatic amplifying hosts.
review_notes: Group-level livestock assignment only.

evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:WHO Crimean-Congo haemorrhagic fever fact sheet
assignment_basis: WHO describes human infection from ticks or infected animal blood and tissues.
review_notes: Spillover/incidental human role with nosocomial caveat.

evidence_record_ids: host_role_evidence:WHO Crimean-Congo haemorrhagic fever fact sheet
assignment_basis: WHO describes ostriches as susceptible in endemic areas.
review_notes: Susceptible host row only; not generalized to all birds.

assignment_basis: CDC Yellow Book describes small rodents as primary amplifying hosts.
